mainTopic: דין מסותא והקדשה · שנים אדוקים בטלית · שנים אדוקים בשטר
overview: (1) The Gemara discusses whether disputed property (מסותא) can be consecrated, concluding that it refers to real estate which remains in the owner's domain. (2) The Gemara analyzes the division of a disputed cloak, discussing the role of an oath and the specific case of a golden cloak. (3) The Gemara discusses the laws of a disputed document found in the market, analyzing the opinions of רבי, רבי שמעון בן גמליאל, and רבי יוסי regarding its validation and return. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Gemara discusses whether disputed property (מסותא) can be consecrated, concluding that it refers to real estate which remains in the owner's domain.
The Gemara analyzes the division of a disputed cloak, discussing the role of an oath and the specific case of a golden cloak.

The Gemara discusses the laws of a disputed document found in the market, analyzing the opinions of רבי, רבי שמעון בן גמליאל, and רבי יוסי regarding its validation and return. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: The principle of עֲשִׂירִי וַדַּאי
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara establishes the principle that the Torah requires a certain tenth animal for tithing, not an uncertain one, applying this to the case of a counted animal jumping back.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא states a foundational principle derived from the תורה regarding tithing. It establishes that only a definite tenth animal is subject to the law.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The text asserts that the תורה mandates a certain tenth (עֲשִׂירִי וַדַּאי) and excludes an uncertain one (עֲשִׂירִי סָפֵק). This implies that if the status of the animal is in doubt, the entire flock is exempt from tithing.
steps.whatToRemember: The principle of עֲשִׂירִי וַדַּאי אָמַר רַחֲמָנָא וְלֹא עֲשִׂירִי סָפֵק is the basis for the exemption.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes the baseline הלכה that uncertainty in the count or status of the animal exempts the flock from tithing.

phrases.english: Rather, what do you have to say?

phrases.aramaic: עֲשִׂירִי וַדַּאי אָמַר רַחֲמָנָא וְלֹא עֲשִׂירִי סָפֵק.
phrases.english: A certain tenth the Merciful One said, and not an uncertain tenth.
Here too:

phrases.aramaic: עֲשִׂירִי וַדַּאי אָמַר רַחֲמָנָא וְלֹא עֲשִׂירִי סָפֵק.
phrases.english: A certain tenth the Merciful One said, and not an uncertain tenth.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא מַאי אִית לָךְ לְמֵימַר, עֲשִׂירִי וַדַּאי אָמַר רַחֲמָנָא וְלֹא עֲשִׂירִי סָפֵק.

rashi.hebrew: אלא מאי אית לך למימר - למה לא חייבוהו לעשר ממה נפשך:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's question 'מה נפשך' implies that regardless of whether the animal that returned was the tenth or not, the person should have been obligated to tithe, making the principle of עֲשִׂירִי וַדַּאי necessary to resolve the doubt.

rashi.hebrew: עשירי ודאי - הצריך הכתוב לעשר ולא הצריך לעשר את הספק וכל עשירי שבכאן ספק הן שאפילו יצא הפטור בחמישי שוב אין העשירי קרוי עשירי אלא תשיעי שהפטור אינו מן המנין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the Torah requires a definite tenth (עֲשִׂירִי וַדַּאי) and excludes a doubtful tenth (עֲשִׂירִי סָפֵק), explaining that in this case, even if the exempt animal left fifth, the tenth animal becomes a ninth, thus all animals here are considered doubtful.

tosafot.hebrew: ולא עשירי ספק - אין להקשות איצטריך קרא למעוטי ספיקא כדפריך במסכת חולין (דף כב: ד"ה איצטריך):
tosafot.english: [The tithe must be a definite tithe] and not a dubious tithe. The verse is teaching us that the mitzvah of tithing is in effect only when the counting process will yield a definite ‘tenth’. If the status of the ‘tenth’ will be dubious, there is no requirement to go through the tithing procedure. We see that the Torah addresses the possibility of a doubt. Elsewhere, (Yoma 74a and Chulin 22b) when the Gemara discusses a verse that deals with a doubt, the Gemara asks: Do we need a verse to deal with a doubt? The presumption of this question is that Hashem knows the reality and has no doubts. Why then would the Torah address a situation of doubts? But our Gemara is telling us that we have a verse to deal with a doubt. Shouldn’t the Gemara here also ask: Do we need a verse to deal with a doubt? Tosfos tells us: We should not ask: Do we need a verse to exclude a tithing obligation when the outcome is in doubt? As we explained in Maseches Chulin (22b. Tosfos איצטריך.) Tosfos there explains that there are two different types of doubts: a) A doubt that is the result of varying circumstances. For example: In our case of the “counted” sheep that jumped back into the corral, there are two possibilities. As we count any group of ten that exits the corral, it is possible that the previously counted one is among them and the tenth is not truly a tenth. It is also possible that the previously counted one is not among the group and the designated tenth is truly a tenth. The truth will vary from situation to situation. The answer is not always the same. b) The circumstances are always the same, but our doubt is about the rule that governs this situation. Such as in the Gemara (Chulin 22b), where the Gemara is dealing with the maturity of pigeons. Pigeons are usable as a sacrifice only while young. Doves are usable only when they are older. There is a doubt about when the feathers start changing colors. The Gemara rules that for the pigeons that period is too old and for the doves it is too young. The Gemara cites a verse to prove this point, but then asks: Do we need a verse to deal with a doubt? Tosfos there (Chulin 22b) explains: In situation a) where there is no standard solution because the facts of the case vary, the Torah must tell us how to deal with a doubt. That is why the Gemara here does not ask: Do we need a verse to instruct us about doubts? Indeed, we do need a verse to tell us how we must deal with a situation when doubts arise. However, in situation b), the status of the birds whose color is changing does not vary from bird to bird, the reality is always the same. The Torah has no doubts and knows whether it is an older or younger bird. It is we, who are unaware of the proper Torah ruling in this matter who have a doubt. We cannot say that the Torah wrote a specific verse to address this situation. The Gemara in (Chulin 22b) therefore asks: Do we need a verse to deal with a doubt?
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses why the Gemara doesn't ask if a verse is needed to exclude a doubtful tithe, as it does in חולין, by distinguishing between doubts arising from varying circumstances (like the sheep jumping back) and doubts about the governing rule, noting that the Torah addresses the former type of doubt.

steps.title: רַב אַחָא's question about סְפֵקוֹת
steps.stepSummary: Rav Acha asks Ravina what type of uncertain animals are subject to tithing according to the Mishnah.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב אַחָא poses a clarifying question to רַבִּינָא regarding the specific type of uncertainty mentioned in the משנה. He asks what category of סְפֵקוֹת the text refers to.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב אַחָא suggests the possibility of סְפֵק בְּכוֹרוֹת (uncertain firstborns). He argues that if this were the case, the sanctity of the firstborn would already apply, making tithing impossible since the תורה says 'it shall be holy'.
steps.whatToRemember: The question is about the specific type of סְפֵקוֹת in the משנה.
steps.whyThisMatters: This question sets up the discussion about the nature of the uncertainty and the applicability of tithing.

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב אַחָא מִדִּפְתִּי לְרָבִינָא: מַאי סְפֵיקוֹת?
phrases.english: Rav Aḥa of Difti said to Ravina: What are these uncertainties?
If we say that it is uncertainty of firstborns,
"It shall be sacred" the Merciful One said,
and not one that is already sacred.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב אַחָא מִדִּפְתִּי לְרָבִינָא: מַאי סְפֵיקוֹת?

rashi.hebrew: מאי ספיקות - דאמרן לעיל הספיקות נכנסין לדיר להתעשר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the term ספיקות refers back to the preceding Mishnah's statement that items of uncertain status enter the enclosure to be tithed.

steps.title: The answer: סְפֵק פִּדְיוֹן פֶּטֶר חֲמוֹר
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara answers that the Mishnah refers to uncertain redemption of a firstborn donkey, citing Rav Nachman's ruling.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא provides the answer to רַב אַחָא's question, identifying the specific type of uncertainty. It clarifies that the משנה refers to uncertain redemption of a firstborn donkey.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The answer cites רַב נַחְמָן's ruling that an Israelite with ten uncertain firstborn donkeys must separate ten lambs for redemption and tithe them. This implies that the uncertainty does not confer sanctity, allowing tithing to apply.

keyTerms.meaning: Uncertain redemption of a firstborn donkey

steps.whatToRemember: The משנה refers to סְפֵק פִּדְיוֹן פֶּטֶר חֲמוֹר, not סְפֵק בְּכוֹרוֹת.
steps.whyThisMatters: This distinction is crucial for understanding the applicability of tithing in cases of uncertainty.

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא סְפֵק פִּדְיוֹן פֶּטֶר חֲמוֹר, וְכִדְרַב נַחְמָן.
phrases.english: Rather, an uncertain redemption of a firstborn donkey, and according to Rav Naḥman.

phrases.aramaic: דְּאָמַר רַב נַחְמָן אָמַר רַבָּה בַּר אֲבוּהּ:
phrases.english: That Rav Naḥman says that Rabba bar Avuh says:

phrases.aramaic: יִשְׂרָאֵל שֶׁיֵּשׁ לוֹ עֲשָׂרָה סְפֵק פִּטְרֵי
phrases.english: An Israelite who has ten uncertain firstborn donkeys
in his home –
separates ten lambs for them,
and tithes them, and they are his.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא סְפֵק פִּדְיוֹן פֶּטֶר חֲמוֹר, וְכִדְרַב נַחְמָן.

rashi.hebrew: ספק פדיון פטר חמור - טלה שהופרש על ספק פטר חמור שאפילו הוא פטר חמור ודאי אין בטלה זה של פדיון משום קדושה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the term ספק פדיון פטר חמור refers to a lamb designated for a questionable firstborn donkey, which does not acquire sanctity even if the donkey turns out to be a definite firstborn.

rashi.hebrew: עשרה שיין - לאפקועי איסורייהו למשרי להו בעבודה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the purpose of separating ten lambs is to remove the prohibition of sanctity from them, thereby permitting their use for work.

tosafot.hebrew: מפריש עליהן עשרה שיין - תימה דבפ"ק דבכורות (דף יא. ושם) אמרי' דשה אחד פוטר כמה פטרי חמורים ומה צריך עשרה שיים ותו מאי קא משמע לן מתני' היא הספיקות נכנסים לדיר להתעשר ותו כיון דאמר מעשרן מה צריך להשמיענו דהן שלו פשיטא כיון דמעשרן דהן שלו דאילו היו של כהן לא היה מעשרן דהוו להו כלקוח דתנן (שם דף נה:) הלקוח והניתן לו במתנה פטור ואי קמ"ל דאע"ג דמעשרן עדיין הם שלו פשיטא דמעשר בהמה של בעלים הוא:
tosafot.english: [One who has ten questionable firstborn donkeys] designates ten sheep to exempt them. [He then tithes the ten sheep and keeps them]. In order to understand this Tosfos we must study a small portion of a Mishna in Bechoros 9a: After discussing all the types of sheep that may be used for the redemption of a firstborn donkey, the Mishna concludes: One may redeem a firstborn donkey with a single sheep many times. A simple reading of this sentence seems to be saying that even if one has many firstborn donkeys to redeem, he may use the same sheep and he need not use one sheep per firstborn donkey. This, however, is simply not true. A bit earlier the very same Mishna teaches us: A Yisrael who has two female donkeys that have not yet calved, and they had two males, he must give two sheep to a Kohen. It is obvious that one cannot fulfill his obligation by redeeming two donkeys with one sheep. If so, how are we to understand the Mishna that tells us: One can redeem with [a sheep] many times. There is a difference of opinion about this: a) Rashi Bechoros 9a ד'ה ופודה בו פעמים הרבה says: If the Kohen returned the sheep to the Yisrael after he redeemed the firstborn donkey with that sheep, the Yisrael may use that very same sheep to redeem another firstborn donkey. A single sheep may not be used as a redemption for many firstborn donkeys at one time. The Mishna is only saying that it may be used for a second redemption when it is returned to the Yisrael’s possession. b) Rabbeinu Tam says: When the Mishna says that one can use the same sheep for many redemptions, the Mishna is speaking of when the sheep was used to redeem a questionable firstborn donkey. In such cases the redemption is done by designating a sheep in place of the questionable firstborn donkey, but it need not be given to the Kohen, since there is a doubt as to whether the Kohen is entitled to receive the sheep. The sheep designated as the redemption can be designated again and again, since it need not be actually given to the Kohen. A proper analysis of this material will yield that Tosfos’ upcoming question is valid only according to Rabbeinu Tam, but not according to Rashi. (See Tosfos Bechoros 11a, ד'ה שהיו לו, who states this clearly. Also see Artscroll Bechoros 4b3, note 21.) Tosfos asks three questions that he cannot resolve. The first: This is bewildering! For in the first perek of Bechoros (11a) [the Gemara] says that one sheep exempts many firstborn donkeys. If so, so why are ten sheep needed? We can exempt all of the questionable firstborn donkeys with only one sheep! The second: Furthermore, what is [Rav Nachman in the name of Rabah bar Avuha] teaching us? Ultimately, this is the lesson of the Mishna: The dubious [firstborn donkeys] are gathered into the corral to be tithed. When the Gemara searches for an explanation of this Mishna in Bechoros 9a: “The questionable sheep are gathered into a corral to be tithed”, it concludes that the Mishna is teaching us Rabah bar Avuha’s rule. If this is in fact the only viable explanation of the Mishna, it emerges that this is in fact the lesson of the Mishna. If so, why did Rabah bar Avuha need to teach it, we already know this from the Mishna? The third question: Furthermore, since [Rabah bar Avuha] said that [the Yisrael] must tithe them, why does he also need to inform us that they are his property? It is abundantly clear that since [the Yisrael owner] is obligated to tithe them, they must be his property. For if they were the property of the Kohen,1Tosfos seems to be entertaining the possibility that there is truly a doubt as to whether the redemption sheep belong to the Yisrael or the Kohen. However, since we do rule that the Yisrael may keep the sheep because he is in possession, he might be considered the true owner and would be liable to tithe the questionable sheep. Tosfos rejects this understanding because even if we were to say that because of his questionable ownership, he is obligated to tithe the questionable redemption sheep, there would still not be an obligation to tithe this group. The fact that the Yisrael is allowed to retain ownership of the sheep, despite the fact that they may belong to the Kohen, must be viewed as if the sheep were sold to him, since the sheep are not inherently his. It is only because of the ruling that he who is in possession of such items may continue to maintain possession that the Yisrael is permitted to keep the sheep. The right to maintain ownership of these sheep must be viewed as if the sheep were taken from their possibly rightful owner - the Kohen, and given to the Yisrael to keep. This is the equivalent of purchased sheep, which are not subject to the tithing obligation. See משנת הלוי by Harav Raphael Schorr, שליט"א, who discusses this issue. [the Yisrael] would not be required to tithe them, for they would be equivalent to sheep that were purchased. For we have learned in a Mishna in Bechoros (55b): sheep that were purchased or given to [the Yisrael owner] as a gift are exempt from tithing. Thus, there is no need to inform us that the tithed sheep are the property of the Yisrael owner. And if he is informing us that even though he tithed [the group], [the tithe itself] still belongs to the owner, that too is apparent, for the tithe of animals always belongs to the [Yisrael] owner. The Yisrael owner offers it as a sacrifice in the Temple and the flesh is eaten by his family. If the tithe was blemished, the Yisrael owner eats it as regular non-sacrificial meat. [See also Tosfos Bechoros (4b) ד'ה ופודה, and also Bechoros (11a) ד'ה שהיו.]
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות raises a difficulty based on the Mishnah in בכורות, which states that one sheep can redeem multiple firstborn donkeys, questioning why ten lambs are needed and why the Gemara teaches that they belong to the owner, since tithing implies ownership.

steps.title: The question about מַסּוּתָא
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks what the halachic conclusion is regarding disputed property (מַסּוּתָא).
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא transitions to a new topic, asking about the status of disputed property. It introduces the concept of מַסּוּתָא and seeks to determine its הלכה status.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא cites a case involving רַב חִסְדָּא and רַב הוּנָא, who resolved the issue based on רַב נַחְמָן's ruling. They determined that property which cannot be recovered by legal process cannot be consecrated.
steps.whatToRemember: The question is about the status of מַסּוּתָא.
steps.whyThisMatters: This question sets up the discussion about the consecration of disputed property.

phrases.aramaic: מַאי הָוֵי עֲלַהּ דְּמַסּוּתָא? תָּא שְׁמַע,
phrases.english: What was the outcome regarding the bathhouse? Come and hear,
as Rabbi Hiyya bar Avin said:

phrases.aramaic: הֲוָה עוֹבָדָא בֵּי רַב חִסְדָּא וְרַב
phrases.english: There was an incident before Rav Hisda and Rav
Hisda before Rav Huna,
and he resolved it from that which Rav Nachman said:

phrases.aramaic: כׇּל מָמוֹן שֶׁאֵין יָכוֹל לְהוֹצִיאוֹ בְּדַיָּינִין,
phrases.english: All property that one cannot extract through judges,
he consecrated it – it is not consecrated.

rashi.hebrew: עובדא הוה בי רב חסדא ורב חסדא בי רב הונא - מעשה היה ובא לפני רב חסדא ורב חסדא הביאו לפני רב הונא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the case was brought before רב חסדא, who then referred it to רב הונא.

rashi.hebrew: שאינה יכול להוציאו כו' - והאי נמי דחזינן ליה דאין יכול להוציאו בדיינין שאין לו ראיה בדבר אין הקדישו קדוש:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that Rav Nachman's principle applies here because the claimant lacks proof, meaning the property cannot be recovered through legal process and therefore cannot be consecrated.

steps.title: Challenge from רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges Rav Nachman's ruling by citing Rabbi Yochanan's statement that neither the owner nor the robber can consecrate stolen property.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty against רַב נַחְמָן's ruling based on רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's statement. It challenges the inference that recoverable property can be consecrated.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן rules that in a case of theft where the owner has not despaired, neither the owner nor the robber can consecrate the property. This contradicts the inference from רַב נַחְמָן's ruling, as the owner can recover the property but cannot consecrate it.
steps.whatToRemember: The challenge is based on the inference from רַב נַחְמָן's ruling and רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's statement.
steps.whyThisMatters: This challenge highlights a potential contradiction in the הלכה principles regarding consecration.

phrases.english: This one could take it out by judges,
He consecrated it – it is consecrated,
Even though he did not take it out.
And Rabbi Yoḥanan said:
He robbed and the owners did not despair –
Neither of them can consecrate it.
This one – because it is not his,
And that one – because it is not in his possession.

steps.triggerLanguage: הָא יָכוֹל לְהוֹצִיאוֹ בְּדַיָּינִין, הִקְדִּישׁוֹ – קָדוֹשׁ, אַף עַל גַּב דְּלָא אַפְקֵיהּ.

rashi.hebrew: הא יכול להוציאו כו' - בתמיה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's phrase 'הא יכול להוציאו בדיינין' is a rhetorical question challenging the assumption that the owner can recover the stolen item through a court.

rashi.hebrew: שאינו ברשותו - והא סתם גזילה יכולין להוציאו בדיינין הוא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that Rabbi Yochanan's statement 'שאין ברשותו' refers to the robber's lack of ownership, even though the owner technically has the legal ability to recover the item through a court.

steps.title: The resolution: מַסּוּתָא מְקַרְקְעֵי
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the difficulty by distinguishing between movable and immovable property.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא provides a resolution to the difficulty raised in the previous step. It distinguishes between immovable and movable property.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The resolution is that the case of מַסּוּתָא refers to immovable property (מְקַרְקְעֵי), not movable property (מִטַּלְטְלִין). In the case of immovable property, once the owner can recover it by legal process, it is considered to be in his possession, and thus he can consecrate it.
steps.whatToRemember: The resolution is that מַסּוּתָא refers to immovable property.
steps.whyThisMatters: This distinction is crucial for understanding the applicability of consecration in cases of disputed property.

phrases.english: Did you think that in the bathhouse we are dealing with a movable bath?
In the bathhouse we are dealing with a fixed bath,

phrases.aramaic: דְּכִי יָכוֹל לְהוֹצִיאָה בְּדַיָּינִין, בִּרְשׁוּתֵיהּ קָיְימָא.
phrases.english: Since he can evict it by judges, it remains in his permission.

rashi.hebrew: במסותא מטלטלין - כגון גיגית:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the initial assumption of the Gemara's question was that מַסּוּתָא refers to movable property, such as a גיגית (a large pot).

rashi.hebrew: מקרקעי - דכל היכא דאיתיה ברשות מרי' קיימא דקרקע אינה נגזלת ושפיר פשיטנא מהא דאי הוה ליה ראיה עלה הקדישו הקדש:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the resolution relies on the principle that קרקע אינה נגזלת, meaning immovable property remains in the owner's רשות even when stolen, allowing him to consecrate it if he has proof of ownership.

steps.title: רַב תַּחְלִיפָא's teaching on disputed garments and רַבִּי אַבָּהוּ's addition
steps.stepSummary: Rav Taḥalifa teaches that two people grasping a garment each take what they hold and split the rest, and Rabbi Abbahu adds that this requires an oath.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with a בריתא taught by רַב תַּחְלִיפָא before רַבִּי אַבָּהוּ, establishing the basic rule for two people holding a garment. רַבִּי אַבָּהוּ adds a silent condition.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The בריתא establishes a proportional division based on physical grasp, with the remainder split equally. רַבִּי אַבָּהוּ's gesture (מַחְוֵי לֵיהּ) adds the requirement of an oath (בִּשְׁבוּעָה), implying that without an oath, the division might not be valid.
steps.whatToRemember: The basic rule is proportional to grasp, remainder split equally. רַבִּי אַבָּהוּ adds the requirement of an oath.
steps.confusionAlert: Note that רַבִּי אַבָּהוּ's addition is silent, indicated by a gesture, which is a common תלמוד trope for adding a condition or clarification.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the entire discussion about how physical possession interacts with legal claims and oaths in property disputes.

phrases.aramaic: תָּנֵי רַב תַּחְלִיפָא בַּר מַעְרְבָא קַמֵּיהּ דְּרַבִּי אֲבָהוּ:
phrases.english: Rav Taḥalifa bar Ma'arva taught before Rabbi Abbahu:
Two grasping a garment –

phrases.aramaic: זֶה נוֹטֵל עַד מָקוֹם שֶׁיָּדוֹ מַגַּעַת,
phrases.english: This one takes up to where his hand reaches,

phrases.aramaic: וְזֶה נוֹטֵל עַד מָקוֹם שֶׁיָּדוֹ מַגַּעַת,
phrases.english: and that one takes up to where his hand reaches,
and the remainder they divide equally.
Rabbi Abbahu indicated to him: And with an oath.

steps.triggerLanguage: תָּנֵי רַב תַּחְלִיפָא בַר מַעְרְבָא קַמֵּיהּ דְּרַבִּי אַבָּהוּ:

rashi.hebrew: בר מערבא - מארץ ישראל:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רב תחליפא בר מערבא was from ארץ ישראל.

rashi.hebrew: מחוי ליה - מראה באצבעותיו כלפי מעלה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבי אבהו's מחוי ליה was a gesture pointing upward with his fingers.

tosafot.hebrew: מחוי ר' אבהו והשאר בשבועה - נראה דלא גרסי' והשאר דאף במה שידו מגעת ישבע כיון דנתקנה השבועה שלא יהא אדם הולך ותוקף מטעם זה ישבע אף במה שהוא תופס ומכל מקום שפיר מוכח בסמוך דבקנין מאי דתפיס כמאן דפסיק דמי כיון דהכא לאחר שבועה נוטל כל מה שהוא תופס אפילו יותר ממחצה והשבועה היא בשביל הטענה ומפני התקנה:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות argues that the text should not include והשאר, meaning the שבועה applies even to the portion each person is physically grasping, because the תקנה of שבועה prevents people from forcefully taking items, and the שבועה is required due to the claim and the תקנה.

steps.title: Challenge: How does the משנה's ruling fit with the בריתא?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges how the Mishnah's ruling of equal division can be reconciled with the baraita's proportional division based on grasp.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty: the משנה says they split equally, but the בריתא says each takes what they hold. It asks how both can be true.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא identifies a potential contradiction between the משנה (which teaches equal division) and the בריתא (which teaches proportional division based on grasp). The challenge is to find a case where the משנה's ruling applies without contradicting the בריתא.

keyTerms.meaning: How do you find it (a case for it)?

steps.whatToRemember: The משנה teaches equal division, while the בריתא teaches proportional division based on grasp.
steps.confusionAlert: The גמרא is not rejecting the בריתא, but trying to find a case where the משנה's ruling is applicable.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the need to distinguish between different cases of disputed garments.

phrases.english: But our Mishnah, which states that they divide it between them,

phrases.aramaic: וְלָא קָתָנֵי זֶה נוֹטֵל עַד מְקוֹם שֶׁיָּדוֹ מַגַּעַת,
phrases.english: and does not state that this one takes until where his hand reaches,
How do you find it?
Rav Pappa said: That they grasped the fringes.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא מַתְנִיתִין, דְּקָתָנֵי דְּפָלְגִי בַהֲדָדֵי, וְלָא קָתָנֵי זֶה נוֹטֵל עַד מְקוֹם שֶׁיָּדוֹ מַגַּעַת, הֵיכִי מַשְׁכַּחַתּ לַהּ?

rashi.hebrew: בכרכשתא - בגרדין שבשני ראשין שקורין ברניי"ש:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that כרכשתא refers to a vegetable with two ends, allowing each party to hold one end, which reconciles the Mishnah's equal division with the baraita's rule of taking what one holds.

steps.title: רַב מְשַׁרְשְׁיָא's derivation about acquisition with a cloth
steps.stepSummary: Rav Mesharshiyya derives a halakha about acquisition with a cloth from Rav Pappa's statement, stating that grasping three by three fingerbreadths is sufficient for acquisition.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב מְשַׁרְשְׁיָא derives a halakha about acquisition with a cloth (סוּדָרָא) from רַב פַּפָּא's distinction. He concludes that grasping three by three fingerbreadths is sufficient for acquisition.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב מְשַׁרְשְׁיָא argues that grasping three by three fingerbreadths is significant enough to be treated as severed from the rest of the cloth. This allows the verse 'וְנָתַן לְרֵעֵהוּ' to apply, validating the acquisition.

keyTerms.meaning: And he gave to his neighbor

steps.whatToRemember: Grasping three by three fingerbreadths of a cloth is sufficient for acquisition.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes a specific measurement for acquisition with a cloth, which is a key concept in property law.

phrases.english: Rav Mesharshya said:
Learn from it: This cloth,

phrases.aramaic: כֵּיוָן דְּתָפֵיס בֵּיהּ שָׁלֹשׁ עַל שָׁלֹשׁ,
phrases.english: Since he grasped three by three in it,
We read in it "and he gave to his neighbor."
As one who severed its value, and acquires.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַב מְשַׁרְשְׁיָא, שְׁמַע מִינַהּ: הַאי סוּדָרָא, כֵּיוָן דְּתָפֵיס בֵּיהּ שָׁלֹשׁ עַל שָׁלֹשׁ, קָרֵינַן בֵּיהּ ״וְנָתַן לְרֵעֵהוּ״.

rashi.hebrew: ש"מ - מדקתני עד מקום שידו מגעת קנוי לו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the derivation is based on the Mishnah's statement that an item is acquired up to the point where one's hand reaches.

rashi.hebrew: האי סודרא - של קונה שקונין בו קנין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the סודרא mentioned refers to the buyer's cloth used for acquisition.

rashi.hebrew: כיון דתפיס ביה - מקנה שלש על שלש קרינא ביה ושלף איש נעלו ונתן לרעהו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that grasping three by three fingerbreadths of the cloth is sufficient to fulfill the requirement of 'וְנָתַן לְרֵעֵהוּ'.

rashi.hebrew: דכמאן דפסיק דמי - וקנייה מקנה והמכר נקנה בו ללוקח ודוקא נקט שלש על שלש דכלי בעינן דכתיב (רות ד) נעלו ובציר משלש אצבעות לאו בגד הוא כדאשכחן גבי טומאה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that grasping three by three fingerbreadths is considered as if the cloth were severed, allowing for acquisition, and specifies this measurement because a vessel requires a minimum size, as anything smaller is not considered a garment according to the laws of טומאה.

steps.title: Challenge from רַב חִסְדָּא's ruling on a bill of divorce
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges Rav Mesharshiyya's derivation by citing Rav Hisda's ruling that a bill of divorce is not valid if the husband can still pull it from the wife's hand.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty from רַב חִסְדָּא's ruling, which seems to contradict the idea that partial possession is sufficient for acquisition. רַב חִסְדָּא rules that if a husband places a גֵּט in his wife's hand but can still pull it out, she is not divorced.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This ruling implies that as long as the husband has some hold on the גֵּט, it is not considered as if he gave it to her. This challenges רַב מְשַׁרְשְׁיָא's derivation that partial possession is sufficient for acquisition.

keyTerms.meaning: A bill of divorce in her hand and a string in his hand
If he can tear it and bring it to him

steps.whatToRemember: רַב חִסְדָּא rules that a bill of divorce is not valid if the husband can still pull it from the wife's hand.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the need to distinguish between the requirements for acquisition and the requirements for a bill of divorce.

phrases.english: And what is the difference from Rav Hisda?
That Rav Hisda said:
A bill of divorce in her hand and a string in his hand,
If he can tear it and bring it to himself –
She is not divorced, and if not – she is divorced!

steps.triggerLanguage: וּמַאי שְׁנָא מִדְּרַב חִסְדָּא, דְּאָמַר רַב חִסְדָּא: גֵּט בְּיָדָהּ וּמְשִׁיחָה בְּיָדוֹ, אִם יָכוֹל לְנַתְקוֹ וְלַהֲבִיאוֹ אֶצְלוֹ – אֵינָהּ מְגוֹרֶשֶׁת, וְאִם לָאו – מְגוֹרֶשֶׁת!

rashi.hebrew: גט בידה - שנתנו לה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase גט בידה means the husband gave the גט to her.

rashi.hebrew: ומשיחה - שקשור בה בידו אם יש כח באותו חוט שיכול לנתקו לגט מידה ולהביאו אצלו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that משיחה refers to a string tied to the גט that remains in the husband's hand, allowing him to potentially pull it back if the string is strong enough.

rashi.hebrew: אינה מגורשת - אלמא לאו נתינה היא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י concludes that since she is not considered מגורשת when he can pull it back, this demonstrates that such a scenario does not constitute a valid נתינה.

tosafot.hebrew: אם יכול לנתקו - נראה דאם היתה ידה פתוחה בשעת שנתן גט לידה ונשארה המשיחה בידו וקפצה ידה בחוזק שאם מושך המשיחה לא יוכל להביאו אצלו שאינה מגורשת שהרי כשנתן בידה קודם שקפצה ידה היה יכול להביאו אצלו והקפיצה לא עשה הוא אלא היא והוה כטלי גיטך מעל גבי קרקע דאינה מגורשת דלא קרינן ביה ונתן בידה והכא דקאמר אם אינו יכול להביאו אצלו דמגורשת כגון שהיתה ידה קפוצה והוא תחב בידה כל כך בחוזק שאם ימשוך המשיחה לא יביא הגט אצלו ור"ת (נמי) פירש אם הגט כבד כל כך שאם רוצה למשוך הגט אליו תנתק המשיחה מגורשת ומיירי בידה פתוחה ולא קפצה ולא עשתה שום דבר:
tosafot.english: If [the husband] can pull [the bill of divorce and bring it back to himself, she is not divorced]. The Gemara differentiates between acquiring a piece of garment for the purposes of effecting an acquisition and what is needed to effect a divorce. In regard to acquisition Scripture (Ruth 4, 7) uses the verb ונתן and he will give’. Even if a major portion of a garment remains in the hands of the giver, as long as the recipient has three fingers worth in his hands, the three finger section has been given to the recipient. The acquisition is valid. However, in regard to divorcing the Torah uses a much stronger term ‘כריתות - severance’. This requires a greater degree of separation between husband and wife in the act of divorcing. If the husband controls the bill of divorce to any degree, the divorce is invalid. The divorce document must be delivered to the wife in a manner which we will describe as irrevocable. If the husband can bring the bill of divorce back to himself, the divorce is invalid. The term used in our Gemara, ‘if he can pull the string and bring [the divorce document] back to himself’ is somewhat vague. Tosfos attempts to clarify the issue to some degree. מהר"ם explains that initially Tosfos assumes that one can pull the bill of divorce via the string when her hand is open. He cannot pull the string and bring it back when her hand is clenched. But this explanation generates a difficulty, as we will soon see. It appears that if her hand was open when he placed the bill of divorce into her hand, and the string remained attached so that at that point in time he could pull the string and bring the bill of divorce back, and she then clenched her hand tightly, so that if he pulls the string he will not be able to bring the bill of divorce to himself, she is not divorced even though she is presently in total control of the bill of divorce. Why not? For when he placed the bill of divorce in her hand before she clenched her hand, he was able to bring [the bill of divorce] to himself. But now after she clenched her hand, he cannot bring the bill of divorce to himself? The divorce should be valid since he no longer controls the bill of divorce. The clenching of her hand which prevents him from pulling the string and bringing the bill of divorce back to himself, is not the result of his action, rather, it is a result of her action. We cannot view this ultimate result as the husband delivering the divorce document to his wife in a way that is irrevocable. His action did leave the bill of divorce susceptible to being pulled back. It is her clenching of her hand that prevents him from pulling the bill of divorce back. [This case] is comparable to a husband who tells his wife pick up your bill of divorce from the ground, where we rule that she is not divorced, because we cannot say that this method of giving the divorce document that fulfills the requirement of the verse - and he will give the bill of divorce into her hand. When he places the bill of divorce on the ground and she picks it up, he did not give her the bill of divorce, rather she lifted it herself. So too, when the husband places it into her hand, but remains in control because the string is attached, when his wife then takes control by clenching her hand, she has taken the bill of divorce. It was not given to her and the divorce is invalid. If when she clenches her hand the divorce is invalid, when is the divorce effective? Remember מהר"ם says Tosfos that assumes if her hand remained unclenched, it would always be possible to pull the bill of divorce back. Here, where [Rav Chisda] says: if he cannot bring [the bill of divorce] back to himself, she is divorced, he is speaking of a situation where her hand was clenched tightly and he pushed [the bill of divorce] into her clenched hand with such force, that if he would pull the string, he could not bring it back to himself. In this case the irrevocable act of delivery is accomplished completely by the husband who placed it into her tightly clenched hand. She contributed nothing and therefore the divorce is effective. But is this really true? She definitely did not actively contribute to the irrevocable delivery of the divorce. But the status of the delivery as irrevocable is very much dependent upon her continuing to clench her hand. Can we attribute the irrevocable delivery of the bill of divorce exclusively to her husband? This question is the subject of much debate. Rabbeinu Tam may disagree with Tosfos’ conclusion. It seems that he maintains that even when she continues to keep her hand clenched, it is as if she is contributing to the irrevocable delivery of the bill of divorce and that too is ineffective. After all, she must continue to clench her hand in order to prevent her husband from bringing it back to himself. There are those who argue that Rabbeinu Tan does not necessarily disagree with Tosfos, he simply offers a different explanation of the Gemara’s condition, ‘if he can pull the string and bring the bill of divorce back’. This depends on the text of the sentence in Tosfos we are about to learn. Rabbeinu Tam (also)1If we have the word ‘also’ in the text of Tosfos, Rabbeinu Tam is in agreement with the first explanation of Tosfos, that placing the bill of divorce into a clenched hand satisfies the requirement that she must only act as a recipient of the divorce. If we delete the word ‘also’, Rabbeinu Tam is disagreeing with the previous explanation. He is saying that even her passive act of maintaining her hand clenched is considered as - taking her divorce, and the divorce would be invalid. A case of not being able to pull the divorce with a string is possible only when the bill of divorce is so heavy that it cannot be pulled by a puny string. See Gittin 78a Tosfos אם יכול לנתקו ולהביאו כו for further clarification. explained: if the bill of divorce is so heavy that if he wants to pull the divorce document to himself, the puny string will break, she is divorced because his delivery of the divorce document is irrevocable. [Rav Chisda] is speaking of when her hand is open, not clenched and she did absolutely nothing to keep the document in her hand.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the requirement for a גט is stricter than for a regular acquisition because the Torah uses the term כריתות, necessitating complete separation, and clarifies the scenarios of the husband's ability to pull the גט back based on whether the wife's hand is open or closed and the strength of the משיחה.

steps.title: Resolution: A bill of divorce requires complete severance, while acquisition requires giving
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the difficulty by explaining that a bill of divorce requires complete severance, while acquisition requires an act of giving, which can be partial.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא provides a resolution by distinguishing between the requirements for a bill of divorce and the requirements for acquisition. It explains that a bill of divorce requires complete severance (כְּרִיתוּת), while acquisition requires an act of giving (נְתִינָה).
steps.deeperAnalysis: In the case of a bill of divorce, as long as the husband has some hold on the גֵּט, there is no complete severance. In contrast, in the case of acquisition, there is a valid act of giving even if only part of the cloth was given. This resolves the apparent contradiction between רַב מְשַׁרְשְׁיָא's derivation and רַב חִסְדָּא's ruling.
steps.whatToRemember: A bill of divorce requires complete severance, while acquisition requires an act of giving, which can be partial.
steps.whyThisMatters: This distinction allows both רַב מְשַׁרְשְׁיָא's derivation and רַב חִסְדָּא's ruling to be valid in their respective cases.

phrases.english: There, we require excision, and it is not there.
Here, we require giving, and this is here.

steps.triggerLanguage: הָתָם כְּרִיתוּת בָּעֵינַן, וְלֵיכָא. הָכָא נְתִינָה בָּעֵינַן, וְהָא אִיכָא.

rashi.hebrew: כריתות - הבדלה שיהיו מובדלים זה מזה והרי אגודים הם בחוט זה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that כְּרִיתוּת requires complete separation between the parties, which is impossible when the bill of divorce remains tied to the husband by a thread.

steps.title: רָבָא's ruling on a golden garment
steps.stepSummary: Rava rules that even if the garment is golden, they divide it equally.
steps.whatsHappening: רָבָא introduces a new case: a garment with gold thread. He rules that they divide it equally.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רָבָא extends the discussion to a case where the garment is golden. He rules that even in this case, they divide it equally. This seems to imply that the value of the garment does not affect the ruling.
steps.whatToRemember: רָבָא rules that even a golden garment is divided equally.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the need to explain why רָבָא's ruling is not obvious.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רָבָא: אִם הָיְתָה טַלִּית מוּזְהֶבֶת – חוֹלְקִין.
phrases.english: Rava said: If the garment was gold-threaded — they divide.

phrases.aramaic: פְּשִׁיטָא? לָא צְרִיכָא, דְּקָאֵי דַּהֲבָא בֵּי מִצְעֵי.
phrases.english: Self-evident? Not necessary, for the gold is in the middle.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רָבָא: אִם הָיְתָה טַלִּית מוּזְהֶבֶת – חוֹלְקִין.

rashi.hebrew: אם היתה טלית מוזהבת - כלומר אפילו היא מוזהבת חולקים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבה's ruling that they divide the garment applies even when it is golden, meaning the value does not change the law.

steps.title: Challenge: This too is obvious
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges that even in the case where the gold is in the middle, it is obvious that they would divide it equally.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a new challenge, arguing that even in the case where the gold is in the middle, it is obvious that they would divide it equally.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא argues that even in the case where the gold is in the middle, it is obvious that they would divide it equally, as the gold is not in either party's grasp. This challenge sets up the need to explain why רָבָא's ruling is still necessary.
steps.whatToRemember: The גמרא challenges that even in the case where the gold is in the middle, it is obvious that they would divide it equally.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the need to explain the specific case that רָבָא is addressing.

phrases.english: This too is obvious!
It is not necessary [to teach] when it is close to one.

phrases.aramaic: מַהוּ דְּתֵימָא דַּאֲמַר לֵיהּ: פְּלוֹג הָכִי,
phrases.english: What is it that you say that he says to him: Divide it this way,
He teaches us that he says to him:

phrases.aramaic: מַאי חָזֵית דְּפָלְגַתְּ הָכִי? פְּלוֹג הָכִי.
phrases.english: What did you see that you divided it this way? Divide it this way.

rashi.hebrew: פלוג הכי - לרוחבה ויהא הזהב לאחד מהם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's hypothetical challenge assumes the parties would divide the gold by its width, leaving the entire gold piece to one person.

rashi.hebrew: קא משמע לן דא"ל כו' פלוג - לאורכה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the Mishnah teaches that even when one party objects to the proposed division, the other can insist on dividing it by its length.

steps.title: The Baraita of Two Holding a Document
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara introduces a baraita discussing a dispute between a creditor and debtor holding a promissory note, presenting the opinions of רבי, רבן שמעון בן גמליאל, and רבי יוסי regarding its validation and return.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא opens with a foundational baraita establishing the legal parameters for a disputed promissory note (שטר) held by both the creditor (מלוה) and debtor (לוה).
steps.deeperAnalysis: This baraita sets up the entire sugya by presenting three distinct halachic positions. רבי requires validation (הקמה) by witnesses. רבן שמעון בן גמליאל mandates splitting the debt (יחלקו). The baraita also introduces a scenario where the note falls into the hands of a judge (דיין), with a dispute between the anonymous Tanna and רבי יוסי regarding whether it retains its presumptive validity (חזקה).

keyTerms.meaning: Validation of a document by witnesses

steps.whatToRemember: The core dispute is about the status of a שטר when the debtor claims repayment (פירעון).
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes the baseline for understanding how the גמרא will reconcile conflicting opinions about the presumption of repayment.

phrases.english: The Sages taught: Two people grasping a promissory note,
the creditor says: It is mine,
and it fell from me and I found it,
and the debtor says: It is yours,
and I repaid it to you –
the promissory note is ratified through its signatories, the words of Rabbi.

phrases.aramaic: רַבָּן שִׁמְעוֹן בֶּן גַּמְלִיאֵל אוֹמֵר: יַחְלוֹקוּ.
phrases.english: Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel says: They divide.

phrases.aramaic: נָפַל לְיַד דַּיָּין – לֹא יוֹצִיאוֹ עוֹלָמִית.
phrases.english: It fell into the hand of a judge – they do not remove it forever.

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי יוֹסֵי אוֹמֵר: הֲרֵי הוּא בְּחֶזְקָתוֹ.
phrases.english: Rabbi Yosei says: It is in its presumptive status.

rashi.hebrew: שנים אדוקים בשטר - המלוה והלוה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the two parties holding the document are the creditor (מלוה) and the debtor (לוה).

rashi.hebrew: שלך הוא ופרעתיו לך - והחזרת לי וממני נפל:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the debtor's claim of repayment implies the creditor returned the document to him, from which it subsequently fell.

rashi.hebrew: יתקיים השטר בחותמיו - קס"ד דהכי קאמר אם השטר כשר שיקיימוהו חותמיו לומר כתב ידינו הוא הרי הוא בחזקתו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י interprets רבי's requirement for validation (הקמה) as establishing the document's validity through the witnesses' testimony, thereby preserving its presumptive status (חזקה).

rashi.hebrew: נפל השטר ליד הדיין - משמע דהכי קאמר: אם מצאו דיין לא יוציאו אותו עולמית מיד דיין ולקמן פריך מאי שנא דיין מאינשי דעלמא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י notes that the baraita's statement about a judge (דיין) implies the document cannot be permanently removed from the judge's possession, setting up a subsequent question about why a judge differs from ordinary people.

steps.title: Challenge from the Mishnah of Two Holding a Garment
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges רבי's ruling that the creditor collects the full amount after validation, citing a Mishnah where רבי rules that two holding a garment must split it.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty against רבי's position in the baraita. If רבי requires validation, why does he allow the creditor to collect the full amount (גבי ליה מלוה כוליה)?
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא assumes that רבי follows the principle of יחזקו (two holding a garment) from the Mishnah, which mandates splitting the disputed item. Therefore, even after validation, רבי should require the debt to be split, not collected in full. This attacks the internal consistency of רבי's ruling.

keyTerms.meaning: The law of two holding a garment
The creditor collects the entire debt

steps.whatToRemember: The challenge assumes רבי holds יחזקו applies to all disputed holdings.
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces the גמרא to define the precise legal effect of validation (הקמה) according to רבי.

phrases.english: The Master said: The promissory note must be ratified through its signatories.
And he collects from him the entire debt.
And does he not have our Mishnah?
phrases.notes: Refers to the Mishnah in Bava Metzia about two people holding a garment.

phrases.english: Two people holding, etc.!

steps.triggerLanguage: אמר מר: יתקיים השטר בחותמיו. וגבי ליה מלוה כוליה. ולית ליה מתניתין?

rashi.hebrew: וגבי ליה מלוה כוליה - בתמיה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase וגבי ליה מלוה כוליה is read as a rhetorical question, challenging how רבי can permit full collection when his own principle of יחזקו would require splitting the debt.

steps.title: רָבָא's Resolution: Dispute is Only When Unvalidated
steps.stepSummary: Rava, citing Rav Nachman, resolves the difficulty by stating that the dispute between רבי and רבן שמעון בן גמליאל only applies when the document is not validated; once validated, everyone agrees they split.
steps.whatsHappening: רָבָא provides a resolution to the קשיא by redefining the scope of the dispute. He asserts that when the document is validated (מקוים), all opinions agree that the parties split the debt.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This תירוץ resolves the conflict by limiting רבי's ruling to the pre-validation stage. The גמרא is essentially saying: רבי's statement 'יתקיים השטר' implies that *until* it is validated, the creditor gets nothing. Once validated, the standard rule of יחזקו applies, and they split. This explains why the baraita mentions validation: it's the prerequisite for splitting, not for full collection.
steps.whatToRemember: The dispute is about the status of an unvalidated document.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes that validation is a necessary step before any division can occur.

phrases.english: Rava says that Rav Naḥman says:
In a ratified case – everyone agrees they divide,
But they disagree in an unratified case.

steps.triggerLanguage: אמר רבא אמר רב נחמן: במקוים – דברי הכל יחלקו

rashi.hebrew: במקוים - שכתוב בו הנפק שיצא כבר בבית דין והעידו עדים על חתימת ידן וכותבין הדיינין בו שטרא דנן נפק קדמנא ואסהידו פלוני ופלוני על חתימת ידייהו ואשרנוהו וקיימנוהו כדחזי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines מקוים as a document where witnesses testified before a בית דין regarding the signatures, and the judges wrote a certification on the document confirming its validity.

rashi.hebrew: כולי עלמא לא פליגי דיחלוקו - דכי אמרינן דיתקיים השטר בחותמיו לא למגבי מלוה כולו קאמר אלא למגבי פלגיה ופלוגתייהו כשאינו מקוים רבי סבר אף על פי שהלוה מודה ליה שכתב שטר זה ולוה לו מעות צריך המלוה לקיימו בחותמיו ואפילו השטר בידו נאמן הלוה לומר פרעתיך דכל זמן שאינו מקוים מאן קמשוי ליה שטרא לוה שמודה לו שכתבו הא אמר דפריע אבל משהשטר מוחזק על פי עדיו אין הלוה נאמן לומר פרעתי הלכך זה ששניהם מוחזקים אי מקיים ליה מלוה בעדות ומשוי ליה שטר מעליא אין הלוה נאמן באמירתו אלא על ידי חזקה זה שאדוק בו והרי הוא כשאר מציאה ופליג:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that when the document is מקוים, all agree they split the debt because the creditor can only collect half; however, when it is not מקוים, רבי requires validation even if the borrower admits writing it, because the borrower is believed to claim payment until the document is validated by witnesses, at which point the borrower loses that credibility and the standard rule of splitting applies.

steps.title: רבי's View: Unvalidated Document is Merely a Shard
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains רבי's reasoning: an unvalidated document is legally worthless (חספא בעלמא), and since the debtor admits writing it but claims repayment, the document cannot be used without validation.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא explains רבי's underlying principle regarding unvalidated documents. רבי holds that a debtor's admission does not suffice for collection; formal validation is required.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא clarifies that רבי views an unvalidated שטר as חספא בעלמא. The debtor's admission is irrelevant because he simultaneously claims repayment. Without witness validation, the document has no legal force, justifying why רבי insists on validation before any splitting.

keyTerms.meaning: Admits that he wrote the document

steps.whatToRemember: רבי holds that admission alone does not validate a document.
steps.confusionAlert: The term חַסְפָּא בַּעַלְמָא is a strong legal dismissal of the document's status.
steps.whyThisMatters: This clarifies רבי's strict stance on document validation.

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי סָבַר: מוֹדֶה בִּשְׁטָר שֶׁכְּתָבוֹ – צָרִיךְ לְקַיְּימוֹ.
phrases.english: Rabbi holds: One who admits regarding a promissory note that he wrote it — he needs to ratify it.
And if he ratifies it, he divides it,

phrases.aramaic: וְאִי לָא מְקַיֵּים לֵיהּ לָא פָּלֵיג.
phrases.english: and if he does not ratify it, he does not divide it.
What is the reason? It is merely a shard,
phrases.notes: A shard has no legal standing on its own.

phrases.aramaic: מַאן קָא מְשַׁוֵּי לֵיהּ לְהַאי שְׁטָרָא –
phrases.english: Who makes it into this promissory note —
the borrower, and he says that he repaid it.

steps.triggerLanguage: רב סבר: מודה בשטר שכתבו – צריך לקיימו

rashi.hebrew: ואי לא לא פליג - שאפילו כולו ביד המלוה נאמן הלוה לומר פרעתי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that according to רבי, even if the creditor holds the entire document, the debtor is believed to claim repayment because the unvalidated document is merely a shard.

steps.title: רבן שמעון בן גמליאל's View: Admission Suffices for Splitting
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara contrasts רבי with רבן שמעון בן גמליאל, who holds that a debtor's admission validates the document sufficiently for the parties to split the debt, even without formal witness validation.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא presents the opposing view of רבן שמעון בן גמליאל to complete the picture of the dispute. This is a setup line contrasting with רבי.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רבן שמעון בן גמליאל holds that מודה בשטר שכתבו אין צריך לקיימו. The debtor's admission gives the document enough legal weight to proceed to splitting. This explains why he rules יחלקו immediately, without mentioning validation.
steps.whatToRemember: רבן שמעון בן גמליאל holds that admission validates the document for splitting.
steps.confusionAlert: This directly contrasts with רבי's view of חַסְפָּא בַּעַלְמָא.
steps.whyThisMatters: This completes the analysis of the first part of the baraita.

phrases.english: And Rabbi Shimon ben Gamliel holds:

phrases.aramaic: מוֹדֶה בִּשְׁטָר שֶׁכְּתָבוֹ אֵין צָרִיךְ לְקַיְּימוֹ,
phrases.english: He admits regarding a document that he wrote, he does not need to ratify it,

phrases.aramaic: וְאַף עַל גַּב דְּלָא מְקַיֵּים לֵיהּ – יַחְלוֹקוּ.
phrases.english: And even though he does not ratify it — they divide.

steps.triggerLanguage: ורבן שמעון בן גמליאל סבר: מודה בשטר שכתבו אין צריך לקיימו

steps.title: Question Regarding the Judge's Possession
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks why the baraita specifically mentions a case where the document falls into the hands of a judge, implying a unique legal status.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא shifts focus to the second part of the baraita, questioning the specific scenario of a document found by a judge. This is a transition line introducing a new difficulty.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא asks why the case of נפל ליד דיין is singled out. רבא answers that the baraita refers to a third party finding a document previously in the judge's possession. The phrase is interpreted as a document that has already undergone the court's validation process.

keyTerms.meaning: Fell into the hands of a judge
The court wrote 'let it be collected' on it

steps.whatToRemember: The גמרא is trying to understand the specific legal context of the judge's possession.
steps.confusionAlert: רבא's answer reinterprets the phrase נָפַל לְיַד דַּיָּן to mean a document that was validated by the court.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the discussion about the presumption of repayment.

phrases.aramaic: נָפַל לְיַד דַּיָּין לֹא יוֹצִיאוֹ עוֹלָמִית,
phrases.english: If it fell into the hand of a judge, it shall not be removed forever,
What is the difference [in the case] of the hand of a judge?
Rava said, thus he says:

phrases.aramaic: וְאַחֵר שֶׁמָּצָא שְׁטָר שֶׁנָּפַל לְיַד דַּיָּין,
phrases.english: And another [case] where a document fell into the hand of a judge,
And how is it similar –
That it is written in it 'pay',
It shall not be removed forever.

steps.triggerLanguage: נפל ליד דיין לא יוציאו עולמית, מאי שנו ליד דיין?
